speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 4, interview of Adam Boren. August 14, 1996. Adam, while you, your brother, and father were in prison, what did you do to keep up your spirits?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a depressed atmosphere. People who were there didn't know-- some-- some of them didn't know why and where for and what will happen to them. There was a French teacher of mine from the school who had a good sense of humor. And he used to say not ever to worry. Things will be great.

speaker: interviewer
text: In fact, he told a funny, funny story that a Jew, he always has two ways out. And so I ask him, what are the two ways out? And he says, there was a-- a Russian boy who-- who was about to be drafted into the Russian army. And the Russian army, for 25 years, you get into the army. So he says, if they don't draft me, that'll be great. But if they do draft me, I have two ways out. Either they'll take me to the front lines, or to the rear.

speaker: interviewee
text: If they leave me in the rear, that'll be OK. But if they take me to the front lines, I'll have two ways out. Either I kill the enemy or the enemy will kill me. If I kill the enemy, that'll be fine. But if the enemy kills me, I'll have two ways out. Either I go to heaven or I go to hell. If I go to heaven, that'll be great. But if I go to hell, I'll get two ways out. Either I'll eat the devil or the devil will eat me. If I eat the devil, that'll be OK. But if the devil eats me, there'll be only one way out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he kept, you know, telling stories like that. He kept somewhat a-- a more relaxed atmosphere, at least to me. Because he knew me. And about how long did you stay in this jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there for a few weeks. I don't remember exactly how long. But it was the end of January, when at night-- it must have been 12 o'clock, or 11, 12 o'clock-- a-- the doors open and-- and SS men with Ukrainian guards came to the cell and called out half of the cell by name. And we thought perhaps they'll have some kind of a-- a sentence or tell us all about. About an hour or a little more later, they came back and ask everybody out on a run. We got out to the jail. We went-- make us run, with screaming, hitting us with the rifle butts and whips that they had, around the jail's building. And there stood four of other prisoners, naked. Next to them was a-- a hill of clothing. We had to run further. There was a gallow. And four prisoner were hanging from it. And they were dead. Beneath the gallow. They made us grab the dead and drag 'em through the snow to a grave that was dugged out. And then they hung four that were standing naked. And after they hung them, they ran us around the building, toward the undressing area, again screaming and beating. It was dark. It was very, very cold. And it was very windy. As we run, we passed a little shed which was a tool shed. And just by instinct, I jumped into the shed. They hung my brother and my father. I heard the last voices, the prayer Shema Yisrael. And then it became quiet. About half an hour later, they took out the women-- Jewish women's cell. And I could hear the screams and the crying. And they hung them. And became very quiet. There was a-- a hole in the ceiling that was very low. This was not ceiling. The-- the shed had very low ceiling. There was a large hole there. I grabbed that and pull myself up and I was in so-called attic space. It was just a space. There was a space between the roof and the attic, maybe a-- a foot and a half wide. And I look outside and I saw a wall right next to it. And I felt, oh, god. This is a prison wall and I can get on it and get over it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I slid myself to the wall. And it was just a wall of something that they were building inside the jail, unfinished. So I slid off the wall into the snow. And of course-- there were four-- there was a wall around the jail about 20 foot high with four towers in-- on each corner, where there were guards with weapons.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was very quiet. Was very windy. And the wind was playing on a metal roof, making terrible, you know, noises. And the towers were dark. Either they felt that they better off having dark towers, or they fell asleep. I don't know. But when I fell down, I was afraid somebody may have heard it. Because it makes noise. So I lay for a moment, few minutes, and was quiet. And I started looking. There was a wheelbarrow frozen to the ground that didn't have the wheel. It was a wooden wheelbarrow. It goes, two handles and the basket, and the things which holds the wheel. But there was no wheel. I pulled it out from the snow and that made noise again, the crackling noise of the frozen snow. Again, I waited, frozen, my adrenaline working. And I pulled the barrow and put it against the wall. I was right next to the-- to the prison wall. I got on top of it and I slid and fell down. I put the handle down and try to get on top of the wheel wells. And I slid down. I tried twice. Twice, I fell. But looking at it, I see I'll never make the top. It was too low. I kept looking around and I found a bucket, either frozen water or frozen cement in it, frozen in there from the construction site. I pulled that out. Again, I waited. Because in my mind, it make great noise. I put the wheelbarrow against the wall, put the bucket on top of it. Again, I fell. The bucket slid off and I fell. And I felt, I never am going to make it on this side. It's still not high enough. On another-- around the corner of the wall, they must have dumped some dirt against the wall in the construction, that froze, that gave couple more feet height. But it was totally exposed to the jail, while where I was trying was blocked by the partially built building. The other part, where there was a dirt against the wall, was totally enclosed-- totally open. Nonetheless, I took the-- the wheelbarrow. I dragged it through the snow very slowly, put it against the wall, brought the bucket, put it very solidly on the wheel part of the barrow. And slowly, I climbed up. And I grabbed the top of the wall. And I lift myself up. And I was laying on top of the wall, waiting if somebody saw me. And nobody did. And I flipped over and fell on the other side of the wall. I went outside the jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: The jail lay in a open field. I couldn't go across because was white snow. And you could spot me immediately, somebody. So I crawl along the wall to the entrance of the jail. I remember there were trees growing, evergreens, along the road leading to the jail. So I crawl to the-- to the entrance of the jail, and then along the trees, and out. There was a river in town which I knew well, not far away. And I go among the trees till I got to the river. There was a barn. And I hid myself in the barn. I stayed there all day. The following night, I went back to the town. They formed the ghetto. But I went over, there was a wooden-- wooden-- there was-- it was the street closed and there was a wooden fence. That night, I just jumped the fence. And I went to the house of a person that I knew. My father knew him. He was the-- the alteste of the ghetto. What they call that, the-- the-- the ghetto altester, the leader, so to say, that the Germans appointed. He knew my father. He knew me well. And I woke him up. Knock on the window and he came out. He heard about us being arrested. But he didn't know what happen. And I told him what happened, you know, the execution. He-- I was, by the way, I only had a shirt and pants. I had no jacket. My finger were totally frozen, like wood, like pieces of wood. And so were my toes and my ears. It was third degree burn. If you hit it, it was just like you would hit piece of metal.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave me some old clothing and gave me a hat. Because my head was shaved. They-- they shear my hair. And pair of glasses, although I never wore glasses then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And put me into a synagogue in the village, in the town, that was used to shelter people the Germans have thrown out of-- of neighboring villages. They all pushed them to Krzemieniec. And there were hundreds of them laying on the floor there. And I was among them. I was there a few days. And by-- by the way, the alteste-- I don't remember his name-- but after he heard the story, he says, Adam, you gonna survive the war. I don't know where the heck he knew that. But, anyway. I lay there for couple days. And I said, I have to get out. I went back to the-- to him. He says-- I say listen, somehow I have to get out. I have to go back to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he somehow arrange with a guide to get me out of the ghetto, just-- I went on a work kommando. The Jews had to supply working kommandos for the German to wash their trucks, to do other things, the menial thing. And I went in one of the kommando with the guide that got me out. In fact, as I walked through the gate of the ghetto, the Ukrainian guard was the same one that was in jail guard that was during the execution. And of course, my mind, he was looking at me. Although I was looking down. I just noticed him. But to him, probably every Jew looks the same, you know? He didn't notice me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Got out of the ghetto and the guide showed me the roads to lead to the next village with the railroad station. But I hid again, in a-- in a peasant's barn, till nighttime, till evening, till it got dark, and walked to the other village. I had no money. I had no papers. My head was shaved. I waited outside the railroad station. When a train cames in-- he told me there'll be a train. That train goes to Warsaw-- to Lvov. I got on that train and I got to Lvov. I was on a railroad station in Lvov. And this guy told me that there's a great danger to going on a train to Warsaw. Because both the German police, Gestapo, and the Ukrainians search the trains for papers, inspecting papers. And I had nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he doesn't give me-- advise me. But he says it's very dangerous. Be-- be very careful.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was on the railroad station, the loudspeaker announced that in track number five-- and I remember it distinctly-- there is a train from Lvov-- Lemberg, of course, in German-- to Warsaw, only for Germans. [GERMAN]. And I understood Germans from Yiddish a-- a little. And it said, [GERMAN]. Do not board this train. This train is only for Germans. Direct train, Lvov-Warsaw. I-- the trains, the Polish trains had steps outside running the full length of the train. I waited till the train start moving. And I jumped on the steps. And few minutes later, after it left the station, I open the door and walked in. There was a empty cabin. I sat down. Few minutes later-- maybe 10, 20 minutes later-- a German officer came in, sat opposite me, took out his newspaper, open up his attache-- he had a-- a case-- took out a sandwich and start reading, eating. He didn't introduce himself. I didn't introduce myself. We went straight to Warsaw. Being that the train was free for Germans, there were no conductors. There was no inspection. And he didn't know who I was. I knew, more or less, who he was. Truly, I was a little concerned. Because he wore a brown uniform with a band that says, Todt Organisation. Now Todt, to me, T-O-D-T, meant death organization. And he had a swastika, of course, on his left armband. You know, Todt was a worker organization, was a labor-- German labor-- things that build bridges. It's-- the-- it was a German minister, Todt. And his name was Todt. That was-- anyway. Just as the train approach Warsaw railroad, I jumped off the train, before it actually stopped. And I walked [INAUDIBLE] street in Warsaw from the railroad, toward Ogrodowa Street, toward the street I live. There was already wall around Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I saw a column of workers with a-- that they were Jewish. They wore a white armband with a star of David in blue on it, walking toward the ghetto. And I jump in and tell them, can I join with you? I want to get in the ghetto. Of course, they say, stay with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I got into the ghetto. I came home to Ogrodowa 27, to my mother. I was told that my sister was killed in ghetto. She was all alone. I was-- I was really-- I couldn't get myself to tell her what happened to my father and brother. I told her they are-- they are in jail. You know, I couldn't just-- I just couldn't tell her that they were killed. In fact, the family, my grandfather was still there, and some uncles. They got some money together. And there was a Jew who worked for Gestapo-- there were several of those, you know, collaborators. They paid him off to find out what's happen or can he get 'em out of the jail. You know. I didn't wanted to open my mouth and tell them all that's-- there is a waste.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never told anyone.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never told them, no. She was gray. She turned gray, anyway. My mother was a young woman. She was in her 30s at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what month of 1942 would you say you returned to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: In late January, beginning February. Just late January. 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live once you were in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came in, I stayed with my mother. I was out of the-- of the apartment, of the room. Because the apartment was taken over by refugees. You know, there were dozen people living there. The police and the Gestapo went looking for me. Evidently, they found somebody escape. And it was very easy because they saw the-- the-- the wheelbarrow and the bucket against the wall. And there must have been footsteps in the snow, of mine. Somehow, they may have found out from the ghetto, from the leader-- maybe they torture him-- that I escaped. They had our address when they interrogated us originally, of Warsaw, where we lived in Warsaw. And they kept looking for me. So I never came back. They warned me not to come home. And I stayed with my uncle and aunt in other part of the ghetto. I used to see my mother. But only we meet together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a that time that I've changed my birthdates, you know, try to-- to help, in case they try to catch me or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: What date did you start to use for a birthdate?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 10, 1925. Also, being older-- because I was a child still-- thought that perhaps, you know, I have a greater chance they-- with survival with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, during the time that you were in the ghetto, how did you survive in terms of food or work?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My mother sold, piece by piece, everything that we had in our old household. There was a market which-- that items were bought by smugglers who smuggled the items across the wall to the so-called Aryan side of the Warsaw. And then they brought foodstuff back. So this, what we lived in, my mother, no. I don't buy items sold. I didn't work. But I walked around Warsaw. There was-- it was a horrible scene. The scene that it most disturbs me, till today, were tiny babies, two years old, three years old, laying, starving. Laying without parents, parents dead, perhaps. Covered with newspapers. Just whimpering. The most horrible scene.

speaker: interviewer
text: While you were in the ghetto, did you have any contact with any German soldiers who walk through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. None whatsoever. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the uprising, did-- was there ever a time in the ghetto when you thought your life was in danger? Before the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In-- actually, in 1942, in July, the Germans started a-- the action, as it's called. There were signs posted on the buildings that anyone that would voluntarily report himself to so-called Umschlagplatz, which was a railroad siding attached to the ghetto, would get-- would go-- will be transported to a labor camp for work, and will get, I think, two pounds of bread and margarine. Now, first of all, there were thousands, tens of thousand people starving to death. Because the food ration, the card that they got-- they-- you were-- legally got for you food ration, was not enough to live on. You didn't die immediately. You just starve yourself to death with it. Unless you were able to obtain food on the black market, if you had some means, you were dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- those starving people, thousands of them, for the two pounds of bread, they would volunteer to go to hell. And thousands volunteered. But later, there was not enough. And they started-- the-- the Germans, together with the Polish police, and Ukrainians, and a Jewish militia, which-- that was formed in the ghetto by the Judenrat, by the Jewish-- Jewish leadership there-- on order of the Germans-- help in capturing surrounding blocks and telling everybody to get out by the-- or they gonna be killed, shot. And unless they work for a German enterprise and could show documents, they were taken. And families were broken apart. And people start hiding. And of course, that's where the danger came. My uncle, the one that I lived with, he was a manager of a [NON-ENGLISH] or a-- what's [NON-ENGLISH] in English? Sweaters. What is it made of? Wool?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Not wool. How-- how is it made, it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Nylon?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Synthetic?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. They-- they not woven. They are-- there's a name for it. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. Let's go.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a factory where they make socks for the German army. And as such, he gave me and my mother documents that we working for German enterprise. So we were temporary safe. And we stayed-- we didn't stay anymore in our room. We stayed at the factory. So we were safe for a while. So that his family, his wife, and his daughter-- two-- two daughters that he had. Knitwear in-- in-- [NON-ENGLISH] is knitwear. Was factory of knitwear. Before the war, used to make sweaters and other things. And then during the war, the Germans took over the factory, made-- he was the manager-- made socks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's that in July. Then in-- and of course, there was the fear, not knowing what's going on. The Germans cleverly sent back postcards, supposedly by the people they-- that they took.

speaker: interviewee
text: The postcard were preprinted, that say, we are doing well. We are working. The conditions are very comfortable. You know, propaganda stuff like that. But there were some escapees came to ghetto, and says, they are killing us. They have gas chambers. They have used trucks first to gas with carbon monoxide in the trucks. And that there is gas chamber in-- in-- it was in Treblinka and in Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually speak to someone who had escaped, or was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: --told to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --Just gossip. Just-- just-- just-- just the word of mouth from one to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: Word of mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: I never met the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, the first one, when I heard this, would say, he is crazy. It couldn't be. I mean, this is a civilized nation. No matter how brutal they are, this is a nation of Goethe, of Heine, of-- of Beethoven. I mean, how could they murder people, old and young, and women and children, and-- they went crazy. But then a committee send out-- send out-- spies, or whatever, there, from ghetto. And they confirmed it. That what they saw that the transports are going in and nothing coming out. And no people can be seen. That they killing there. And they saw the crematoria, you know, the smoke from crematorium. The stink. The stink is like you burn liver, or you burn meat. Sometimes you burn something in the stove, meat, there's a specific-- or nails that burns, or hair. Burning hair gives you the same smell as cremation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came back and they say, they're killing us. And then people start hiding. And in February 1943-- there were several actions in between. They took-- like 70% of the ghetto was emptied.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two ghettos, actually, in Warsaw. Was a large ghetto and a small ghetto. And they were connected by a bridge over the so-called Aryan site. There were two separate islands. Small ghetto and a street called [NON-ENGLISH], [POLISH] street in Polish, was separating them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after the actions, they just closed up the small ghetto. It was only the large ghetto. And they made it smaller. They-- they evacuated people, whoever was left and could show documents that it's working for German enterprise, into smaller portion of the large ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: The remnants there started hiding. People were buying documents, falsifying documents. The Germans were changing their-- their rules. You could-- you are excluded only if you have documents, x type of document. Then those documents are not valid anymore. Only those documents are valid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the natural reaction of people-- those that they had the right documents, I'm safe, you know? But organization-- a resistance organization start forming. Unfortunately, the old saying, when they are two Jews, they are three parties and four opinions.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were too many parties. There was the-- the Zionist parties, there were the-- the Betar, the Revisionists, the Labor Party, the Hashomer Hatzair. The-- then they were the Socialist Party, the Bund the Communist Party. There were the-- the religious parties. And each one has its own leaders and its own ideas. Finally, finally, they decided to organize. And Anielewicz, the young 21-year-old boy, became the leader of the Jewish fighting force. That's ZOB, Zydowska Organizacja Bojowa, the Jewish fighting organization was form in the ghetto. And in February, the Germans entered the ghetto with armored cars. Ukrainians and Latvians, SS, volunteer SS, and Polish police who were surrounding the ghetto walls. And they were met with fire. With Molotov cocktails and grenades.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at that point when this first fighting took place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was then-- I was then-- no more of Gorgova. Gorgova was taken out of the ghetto. No more. We lived right next to Umschlagplatz, in a little room, just a room, with my mother. It just so happened that fast. They marched in. And they were met with fire. And they withdrew. And quiet prevail. Nothing happened. And the rumor started going-- by the Germans, I'm sure-- that there'll be no more actions. That's it. We stopping. You can work. You can stay here. But the underground did not believe that. We have signs on the streets, by the-- by the organization, by the fighting organization. For instance, stating, hide yourself, don't go voluntarily, don't give in, stay away, prepare some food, prepare some medicine, hide yourself. Also, demanding money. We need money for weapons. There will be a tax. Everyone has to give so much. And those well-to-do-- there are some well-to-do people that were still there-- must give it to-- try to buy weapons. Also, the traitors. They would announce that a collaborator-- Jewish collaborator-- was sentenced by a court martial of the organization to death. The execution will be fulfilled within a week. And sure enough, then the son came in. He was shot.
